food_name: Rice Crackers, Japanese-style, Lightly Salted (Bin Bin / Kameda style)

local_names.romanized_local: Senbei (Japanese); Mǐ Bǐng (Chinese); Keropok Beras (Malay); Arici Paṭṭācu (Tamil); Chāval ke paṭākhe (Hindi)

region_of_origin: Japan (origin); widely consumed in Singapore and Southeast Asia
food_subgroup: Puffed / baked rice snack — made from white rice that is steamed, shaped, and puffed at high heat or pressure

ontology.foodon_label: rice cracker (closest parent: grain-based snack food)
ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific term for Japanese-style puffed rice crackers (senbei). Closest parent is grain-based snack food (FOODON_03301710). No exact URI available; closest_parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice crackers — FoodEx2 category: Grain-based snack foods (closest defensible match within FoodEx2 snack hierarchy)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Japanese-style puffed rice crackers. A039V (grain-based snack) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for rice crackers as a food item in the clinical terminology hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 6 medium round rice crackers, weighing about 30g, as commonly sold in Singapore supermarkets and convenience stores.

nutrient_source.title: Rice crackers, plain, salted — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDCID 167567) cross-referenced with Bin Bin Singapore product label
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy FDCID 167567 'Rice crackers, plain, salted' is an exact category match for plain lightly salted rice crackers of the Bin Bin / Kameda style. Values cross-checked against Singapore Bin Bin product label for sodium and energy confirmation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — rice crackers (plain, puffed)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~80 for plain puffed rice crackers is consistently reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and in Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of GI and GL Values (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Rice crackers are among the highest-GI snack foods tested. GL per 30g serving = (80 × 25.4g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 20.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 570mg/100g; easy to overconsume due to low satiety
hypertension.guidance: At one standard 30g serving, sodium is 171mg (moderate); however, the low fat and protein content provides minimal satiety, making it easy to consume 3–4 servings in one sitting, which can deliver 400–570mg sodium — up to 28% of the 2000mg daily target recommended for hypertension patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic index (~80) and glycemic load (~20 per 30g serving); negligible fat and protein provide no glycemic buffering
type2_diabetes.guidance: Rice crackers are a critical patient misconception item: despite being marketed as 'light' and 'low fat,' their GI of ~80 exceeds that of ordinary cooked white rice (~64), and a single 30g serving produces a glycemic load of ~20 comparable to a full bowl of rice — T2DM patients should avoid rice crackers and substitute lower-GI snacks such as oatcakes (GI ~57) or unsalted nuts (GI ~0–15).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.5g/100g) and zero trans fat are positive; however, high refined carbohydrate load and moderate sodium are concerns for cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While rice crackers are low in saturated fat and contain no trans fat, their high refined carbohydrate content (84.7g/100g, GI ~80) promotes postprandial hyperglycaemia and triglyceride elevation, and their moderate sodium (570mg/100g) contributes to blood pressure load — cardiovascular patients should limit portion size to one serving and prefer fibre-rich, lower-GI alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High glycemic load promotes insulin spikes and pro-inflammatory cytokine release; absence of anti-inflammatory nutrients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The high glycemic load of rice crackers (GL ~20 per 30g) promotes insulin spikes that can elevate pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6); RA patients are better served by anti-inflammatory snacks such as walnuts or seeds that provide omega-3 fatty acids and minimal glycemic impact.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rice crackers (米饼; Japanese: せんべい senbei) are a Singapore dietary paradox — among the most glycemically harmful common snacks, yet widely perceived as a 'healthy,' 'light,' and 'diet-friendly' alternative to biscuits. The product sold in Singapore under the Bin Bin and Kameda brands consists of white rice that is steamed, shaped into round discs, and then puffed at high heat or pressure, which completely destroys the starch granule structure and creates an essentially pure rapidly-digestible starch matrix. The result is a GI of approximately 80 — higher than ordinary cooked white rice (GI ~64) — and a glycemic load of approximately 20 per standard 30g serving (6 crackers), comparable to a full bowl of steamed rice. The macronutrient profile is dominated by carbohydrate (84.7g/100g) with very low fat (2.0g/100g) and modest protein (7.1g/100g), meaning there is essentially no fat, protein, or fibre to moderate glucose absorption speed. Despite this, rice crackers are sold in Singapore with health-adjacent marketing language ('baked not fried,' 'low fat,' 'light and crispy') and are frequently recommended by well-meaning family members to T2DM patients as a diabetes-friendly snack — a clinically dangerous misconception. Sodium is moderate at 570mg/100g (171mg per 30g serving), but the low satiety of the product means overconsumption is common. Correcting the rice cracker misconception is a high-impact clinical counselling point: the contrast with digestive biscuits (GI ~57), oatcakes (GI ~57), and especially almonds (GI ~0) is a memorable and actionable comparison for patients.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDCID 167567 (rice crackers, plain, salted), an exact category match, cross-referenced with Singapore Bin Bin product label. GI value of ~80 is consistently reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008). All major nutrient fields are populated from approved sources.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for plain rice crackers not reported in USDA SR Legacy FDCID 167567 as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the primary source for this product. trans_fat_g is confirmed 0.0 per USDA SR Legacy and product label. cholesterol_mg is confirmed 0.0 — rice crackers are a plant-based grain product containing no animal-derived cholesterol. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_d_mcg, and vitamin_b12_mcg are confirmed 0.0 per USDA SR Legacy FDCID 167567 — these vitamins are naturally absent or negligible in plain puffed white rice crackers. No zero-macro justification required as protein_g (7.1), fat_g (2.0), and carbohydrate_g (84.7) are all non-zero. Input record description field contained an erroneous reference to 'Crispy Indonesian cracker made with peanuts and spices' — this has been corrected; the food is a Japanese-style plain lightly salted puffed rice cracker with no peanuts. FoodEx2 code A039V used as closest defensible parent for grain-based snack; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Japanese-style puffed rice crackers. SNOMED CT has no applicable food concept for this item.
